title: Binary star evolution: a multi-wavelength, multi-messenger puzzle
speaker: Katelyn Breivik
abstract: Recent observations of binary black hole and binary neutron star mergers have ignited interest in the formation and evolution of compact-object binary systems. However, by the time a compact-object binary merges and produces gravitational-wave/electromagnetic signals we can observe, much of the evolutionary history of the stellar progenitors is washed away. By combining binary population synthesis simulations with observations, we can constrain the uncertain processes that govern the evolution of binary stars from zero age main sequence through to compact object formation.

proof_stages.label: Binary-star motivation and multi-messenger program
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames binary stars as a limiting ingredient across astrophysics and lays out the speaker's program of using population synthesis, gravitational-wave catalogs, and astrometric binaries to constrain binary evolution.

proof_stages.label: COSMIC population-synthesis infrastructure
proof_stages.summary: COSMIC is introduced as an open, reproducible binary population-synthesis code based on BSE, integrated with cluster dynamics, equipped with convergence checks, and designed to generate the simulated populations used in the later analyses.

proof_stages.label: LISA double-white-dwarf foreground as a Galactic data source
proof_stages.summary: The speaker argues that Gaia cannot map Galactic white dwarfs beyond the local volume, whereas LISA will see double white dwarfs across the Milky Way and, especially through the unresolved low-frequency foreground, will access the bulk double-white-dwarf population.

proof_stages.label: Spherical-harmonic test of double-white-dwarf Galactic structure
proof_stages.summary: By comparing bright-star and vertically heated double-white-dwarf spatial distributions and projecting the unresolved gravitational-wave foreground into LISA-measurable low-degree spherical harmonics, the analysis estimates that LISA can distinguish sufficiently heated scale heights and compare foreground, resolved, and bright-star structures.

proof_stages.label: Gaia astrometry of compact objects with luminous companions
proof_stages.summary: Compact objects orbiting luminous, noncontact companions are identified as crucial missing intermediate phases of binary evolution, and Gaia astrometry is shown to provide Keplerian dynamical mass information for such unseen companions.

proof_stages.label: Simple forecasts for Gaia black-hole luminous-companion binaries
proof_stages.summary: COSMIC simulations of thin- and thick-disk black-hole luminous-companion populations, with different natal-kick assumptions and observational cuts, identify Gaia's post-interaction discovery space and predict large numbers of observable candidates while exposing simplifying caveats.

proof_stages.label: Realistic Milky-Way refinements and final candidate yield
proof_stages.summary: Adding FIRE/Latte star-formation histories, metallicity dependence, dust reddening, and a minimum black-hole-mass requirement reduces the Gaia forecast to roughly 250--400 black-hole binary candidates, supporting the conclusion that Gaia and LISA will jointly transform binary-evolution constraints.

examples.name: Decadal-survey areas tied to binaries
examples.note: The speaker lists galaxy evolution, stellar evolution, compact-object formation, star and planet formation, planetary false positives, stellar-stream dark-matter searches, and binary-neutron-star multi-messenger events to illustrate the broad importance of binary stars rather than to derive the later methods.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker lists decadal-survey science areas and states how binaries matter for each, but does not compute or check anything on them.

examples.name: Toy Gaia astrometric orbit of a neutron star and solar-mass companion
examples.note: A simulated neutron-star–solar-mass binary with different periods and proper motions makes concrete how Gaia sees parallax, proper motion, and orbital wobble in a compact-object binary.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through the toy Gaia orbit plot, explaining how parallax, proper motion, orbital period, and binary motion shape the observed tracks.
